items.q: When energy is 'lost' as heat due to friction, what has really happened?
items.correct: Energy has been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — it's not destroyed, just dissipated.
items.misconception: Energy has been used up and no longer exists.
items.random_wrong: Energy has been converted into mass following E=mc^2.
items.why: Everyday language ('used up', 'lost') and bills ('energy used') reinforce the idea energy is consumed, contradicting conservation.

items.q: Why do transformers only work with alternating current?
items.correct: A transformer needs a changing magnetic flux in the core; DC produces a steady flux and so no emf is induced in the secondary.
items.misconception: Transformers work with DC too, just less efficiently.
items.random_wrong: DC cannot enter a transformer because the iron core blocks it.
items.why: Students remember transformers as boxes that 'change voltage' and don't link the operation to changing flux being a requirement.

items.q: Why does light bend when it enters a denser medium like glass?
items.correct: Because it slows down — the change in speed at the boundary causes the wavefront to bend (towards the normal).
items.misconception: Because the medium is denser, it physically blocks part of the light and forces it to turn.
items.random_wrong: Because gravity in the glass pulls the light down.
items.why: Students attach refraction to 'density' as a material property without linking it to the change in wave speed and Snell's law.

items.q: A 2 kg object accelerates at 4 m/s². What is the resultant force?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 8 N. “2 N” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does it mean if the resultant force on an object is zero?
items.correct: The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.
items.why: It's The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity.. “The object must be stationary.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Calculate the weight in newtons of a 5 kg mass on Earth (g = 9.8 N/kg).
items.why: The real figure is 49 N. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
